nuance: たら is the most versatile conditional — the second clause follows once the first is realized; works with a single, one-off event.

examples.en: When I arrive at the station, I'll call you.
If I have time, I want to go to Hokkaido.
When my shift ends, let's go eat ramen in Shibuya.

caution: The main clause can't be a stative fact tied to the general present; use と or ば for "whenever X, Y always happens," and keep た-form (着いたら, not 着くたら).

peg.ja: 諦めたらそこで試合終了だよ
peg.romaji: Akirametara soko de shiai shūryō da yo
peg.en: The moment you give up, that's when the game's over.
peg.source: スラムダンク (Slam Dunk) — 安西先生

nuance: ば states a general or logical if-then; the main clause is often a wish or a natural result.

examples.en: If it's cheap, I'll rent this room.
If you practice every day, your Japanese will get better.
If the rent is cheap, I can live in the city center.

caution: Avoid ば when the main clause is a command, request, or invitation (×勉強すれば、遊びに行きましょう) — use たら there instead.

peg.ja: 努力すれば、夢は必ず叶う
peg.romaji: Doryoku sureba, yume wa kanarazu kanau
peg.en: If you put in the effort, your dream will surely come true.
peg.source: NARUTO — ロック・リー (styled)

nuance: なら picks up something the other person just raised — 'if that's the topic, then...'.

examples.en: If it's hot springs (you're after), Hakone is famous.
If you're looking for a cheap room, a share house is good.
If you're going to the ward office, the morning is less crowded.

caution: なら attaches to the plain form WITHOUT past た for a present topic (日本へ行くなら), so don't confuse it with the identical-looking だ→なら of the copula.

peg.ja: 勝ちたいなら、俺の背中を見てろ
peg.romaji: Kachitai nara, ore no senaka o mitero
peg.en: If you want to win, watch my back.
peg.source: ハイキュー!! (Haikyu!!) — 澤村大地 (styled)

nuance: と marks an automatic, always-true result — turn this and that happens. No command or request may follow.

examples.en: When you press this button, a ticket comes out.
When spring comes, the cherry blossoms bloom.
Exit this ticket gate and there's a konbini right on the left.

caution: と cannot be followed by a request, command, permission, or volition in the result clause (×このボタンを押すと、待ってください) — that's the single most common と mistake.

peg.ja: このスイッチを押すと、世界が変わる
peg.romaji: Kono suicchi o osu to, sekai ga kawaru
peg.en: Press this switch, and the world changes.
peg.source: STEINS;GATE — 岡部倫太郎 (styled)

examples.en: Even if it rains, I'll go to work.
Even if it's expensive, I'll buy these shoes.
Even if I miss the last train, I'll walk home.

nuance: Concedes a condition that fails to change the outcome — "X, yet Y anyway." With a question word (誰でも, いくら…ても) it becomes "no matter who/how much."
caution: Don't swap it for the permission 〜てもいい ("may I…"): ても alone means "even if," and needs a following clause, not いい.

peg.ja: 何度倒れても、俺は立ち上がる
peg.romaji: Nando taorete mo, ore wa tachiagaru
peg.en: No matter how many times I fall, I'll stand back up.
peg.source: NARUTO — うずまきナルト (styled)

meaning: come to / reach the point where
connection: V-dict or V-ない + ようになる
nuance: Marks a gradual change ending in a new ability or habit that wasn't there before.

examples.en: I've reached the point where I can read a Japanese newspaper.
I've started running every morning (as a habit).
After half a year, I got to where I could catch the station announcements.

caution: Built on the potential/plain verb, not the ます-stem (読めるようになる, ×読みようになる); with a negative use 〜なくなる rather than ×ないようになる in speech.

peg.ja: 特訓して、少しずつ力を出せるようになった
peg.romaji: Tokkun shite, sukoshi zutsu chikara o daseru yō ni natta
peg.en: Through hard training, I gradually became able to use my power.
peg.source: 僕のヒーローアカデミア (My Hero Academia) — 緑谷出久 (styled)

meaning: make an effort to / see to it that
connection: V-dict or V-ない + ようにする

examples.en: I make an effort to eat vegetables every day.
Please see to it that you're not late for the meeting.
I make sure not to sort the trash the wrong way.

nuance: A deliberate effort or policy to bring about a state — you steer your own behaviour. Contrast ようになる, where the change happens to you on its own.
caution: Don't confuse it with ようになる: ようにする is a conscious effort you make, ようになる is a change that simply comes about.

peg.ja: 毎日休まず、必ず稽古するようにしている
peg.romaji: Mainichi yasumazu, kanarazu keiko suru yō ni shite iru
peg.en: Every day without rest, I make sure to always train.
peg.source: 鬼滅の刃 (Demon Slayer) — 竈門炭治郎 (styled)

nuance: Expresses an intention just decided on; とおもっている describes an ongoing plan.

examples.en: I think I'll go to Akihabara this weekend.
I'm thinking of buying a car next year.
When I get paid, I think I'll buy a new bike.

caution: It must attach to the volitional form (行こうと思う), not the dictionary form (×行くと思う, which just means "I think he goes").

peg.ja: 今日こそ、あいつに告白しようと思う
peg.romaji: Kyō koso, aitsu ni kokuhaku shiyō to omou
peg.en: Today for sure, I think I'll confess to them.
peg.source: 君の名は。(Your Name) — 立花瀧 (styled)

nuance: The casual counterpart of 〜ましょう — used with friends to suggest doing something.

examples.en: Let's drink at the izakaya tonight.
I'm tired, so let's head home soon.
Before the interview, let's grab a coffee or something.

caution: Plain 〜(よ)う is informal "let's/I'll" — use ましょう with strangers or superiors; don't attach と思う unless expressing your own intention.

peg.ja: みんなで最高のチームを作ろう！
peg.romaji: Minna de saikō no chīmu o tsukurō!
peg.en: Let's build the best team together!
peg.source: ハイキュー!! (Haikyu!!) — 日向翔陽 (styled)

examples.en: I'm planning to move house next month.
The meeting is scheduled from three o'clock.
Next month I'm scheduled to go renew my residence card.

nuance: States a fixed, already-arranged schedule — a plan on the calendar, not just a personal wish. Firmer and more objective than つもり.
caution: Nouns take の before 予定 (会議の予定), not だ (×会議だ予定); use it for arranged schedules, not spur-of-the-moment intentions (that's つもり/volitional).

peg.ja: 来週、文化祭を開催する予定です
peg.romaji: Raishū, bunkasai o kaisai suru yotei desu
peg.en: Next week, we're scheduled to hold the culture festival.
peg.source: かぐや様は告らせたい (Kaguya-sama) — 白銀御行 (styled)

connection: plain form + かもしれません

examples.en: It might snow tomorrow.
The store manager might have already gone home.
The Yamanote Line at this hour might be crowded.

nuance: Raises a real possibility the speaker isn't sure of — weaker and less evidence-based than はず or でしょう. Casual form: 〜かも.
caution: Attach it to the plain form; a na-adjective/noun drops だ (静かかもしれない, 雨かもしれない — ×雨だかもしれない).

peg.ja: これは、罠かもしれない
peg.romaji: Kore wa, wana kamoshirenai
peg.en: This might be a trap.
peg.source: 進撃の巨人 (Attack on Titan) — アルミン・アルレルト (styled)

nuance: はず expresses a logical expectation based on evidence — not obligation.

examples.en: He should have arrived at the airport by now.
This train should stop at Shinjuku.
I made a reservation, so the room should be free.

caution: はず is your logical expectation, NOT obligation — don't use it for "should" as advice/duty (that's 〜べき or 〜たほうがいい).

peg.ja: 計算通りなら、勝てるはずだ
peg.romaji: Keisan-dōri nara, kateru hazu da
peg.en: If it goes as calculated, we should be able to win.
peg.source: コードギアス (Code Geass) — ルルーシュ (styled)

nuance: Reports second-hand information; attaches to the plain form, unlike the look-like そう.

examples.en: According to the forecast, I hear it'll rain tomorrow.
I heard that ramen shop is delicious.
I hear a new part-time workplace is opening in the building by the station.

caution: Hearsay そう attaches to the full plain form (雨が降るそうだ, おいしいそうだ) — if you drop the い/る you switch to the "looks like" appearance そう and change the meaning.

peg.ja: 隣のクラスに、転校生が来るそうだ
peg.romaji: Tonari no kurasu ni, tenkōsei ga kuru sō da
peg.en: I hear a transfer student is coming to the class next door.
peg.source: 涼宮ハルヒの憂鬱 (Haruhi) — キョン (styled)

nuance: Describes an impression from what you see; drops the final い of i-adjectives (おいしい→おいしそう).

examples.en: This cake looks delicious.
It looks like it's about to rain any moment.
This job listing looks good — the hourly pay is high.

caution: Appearance そう attaches to the stem (おいしそう, 降りそう); いい and ない become よさそう and なさそう, and it sounds odd describing things you plainly see are true (×髪が長そう for someone visibly long-haired).

peg.ja: このお肉、すごく美味しそう！
peg.romaji: Kono oniku, sugoku oishisō!
peg.en: This meat looks incredibly delicious!
peg.source: 食戟のソーマ (Food Wars) — 幸平創真 (styled)

nuance: A reasoned inference from evidence; more formal than みたい.

examples.en: The road is wet. It seems it rained during the night.
It seems the person next door isn't home.
It seems this train is delayed.

caution: Nouns connect with の (病気のようだ), and na-adjectives with な (元気なようだ) — a bare だ (×病気だようだ) is wrong.

peg.ja: 犯人は、この部屋から逃げたようだ
peg.romaji: Hannin wa, kono heya kara nigeta yō da
peg.en: It seems the culprit escaped from this room.
peg.source: 名探偵コナン (Detective Conan) — 江戸川コナン (styled)

nuance: The casual spoken equivalent of ようだ; also expresses resemblance ('like a…').

examples.en: It seems like he caught a cold.
This room is cold like a fridge.
The packed rush-hour train is just like a sauna.

caution: Nouns and na-adjectives attach directly with NO な/の (子供みたい, 元気みたい — ×子供なみたい); it's casual, so use ようです in polite or written contexts.

peg.ja: あの人、まるでアイドルみたい！
peg.romaji: Ano hito, marude aidoru mitai!
peg.en: That person is just like an idol!
peg.source: けいおん! (K-On!) — 平沢唯 (styled)

nuance: Reports hearsay you judge reliable; after a noun it means 'befitting / typical of'.

examples.en: Apparently a new shop has opened in front of the station.
Today is a warm, spring-like day.
Apparently the trains will stop tomorrow because of the typhoon.

caution: Don't confuse the two らしい: after a plain sentence it's hearsay ("apparently"), but 男らしい / 学生らしい means "befitting a typical man/student" — very different meanings.

peg.ja: 新しいカフェが、駅前にできたらしいよ
peg.romaji: Atarashii kafe ga, ekimae ni dekita rashii yo
peg.en: Apparently a new café opened by the station.
peg.source: けいおん! (K-On!) — 田井中律 (styled)

nuance: The 'suffering passive' often implies the action was unwelcome to the subject.

examples.en: I got my foot stepped on on the train.
I was asked my name by the store manager.
During my shift, I got a talking-to from the manager.

caution: For godan verbs the passive (書かれる) and potential (書ける) differ, but for ichidan verbs they're identical (食べられる = both "is eaten" and "can eat") — rely on context and particles (に vs が) to tell them apart.

peg.ja: また先生に怒られた
peg.romaji: Mata sensei ni okorareta
peg.en: I got scolded by the teacher again.
peg.source: 銀魂 (Gintama) — 坂田銀時 (styled)

nuance: Ru-verbs take 〜られる; casually the ら drops (食べれる). The object usually takes が.

examples.en: I can eat sashimi.
With this app you can buy tickets.
With this commuter pass, I can go anywhere in Tokyo.

caution: The potential object takes が, not を (刺身が食べられる); ら-drop (食べれる, 見れる) is common in speech but marked wrong in formal writing.

peg.ja: 君はヒーローになれる
peg.romaji: Kimi wa hīrō ni nareru
peg.en: You can become a hero.
peg.source: 僕のヒーローアカデミア (My Hero Academia) — オールマイト

nuance: Means both 'make (force)' and 'let (allow)' — context and particles (に/を) disambiguate.

examples.en: The manager made me work overtime.
I let the child play in the park.
The manager had me train the new hire.

caution: For "let/make," an intransitive verb marks the person with を (子供を遊ばせる), but with an を-taking transitive verb the person switches to に (子供に野菜を食べさせる) — mixing them up is the classic error.

peg.ja: 生殺与奪の権を他人に握らせるな！
peg.romaji: Seisatsu yodatsu no ken o tanin ni nigiraseru na!
peg.en: Don't hand the power over your life and death to someone else!
peg.source: 鬼滅の刃 (Demon Slayer) — 冨岡義勇

nuance: Combines causative + passive: someone forced you, and you found it unpleasant.

examples.en: I was made to wait two hours by the department head.
I was forced to do homework every day.
At the welcome party, I was made to chug my drink.

caution: Godan verbs contract 〜させられる→〜される (待たせられる→待たされる), but ichidan and する verbs never contract (食べさせられる, ×食べされる).

peg.ja: 毎日、上司に残業させられる
peg.romaji: Mainichi, jōshi ni zangyō saserareru
peg.en: Every day I'm made to work overtime by my boss.
peg.source: アグレッシブ烈子 (Aggretsuko) — 烈子 (styled)

nuance: A gentle command from a superior to an inferior — parent to child, teacher to student.

examples.en: Go to sleep now.
Eat your vegetables too.
Tidy up the area in front of the register.

caution: It sounds down-talking, so use it only from a superior to a subordinate (parent→child, teacher→student); never say なさい to a boss, customer, or peer.

peg.ja: 早く宿題をやりなさい
peg.romaji: Hayaku shukudai o yarinasai
peg.en: Do your homework, now.
peg.source: クレヨンしんちゃん (Crayon Shin-chan) — みさえ (styled)

nuance: A rough, forceful 'don't' — mostly male speech, warnings, or signs.

examples.en: Don't take photos here.
It's dangerous, so don't run on the platform.
Don't talk loudly on the train.

caution: Prohibition な attaches to the DICTIONARY form (見るな = don't look); the ます-stem + な (見な) is the opposite — a clipped, friendly command to DO it.

peg.ja: 俺の邪魔をするな
peg.romaji: Ore no jama o suru na
peg.en: Don't get in my way.
peg.source: BLEACH — 黒崎一護 (styled)

nuance: Marks completion or, very often, regret over something that happened. Casual: 〜ちゃう.

examples.en: I went and lost my wallet.
I ended up eating the whole cake.
I ended up oversleeping and missing the last train.

caution: The casual contraction depends on the te-form: 〜てしまう→〜ちゃう but 〜でしまう→〜じゃう (読んでしまう→読んじゃう, not ×読んちゃう).

peg.ja: 大事なチケットを、落としてしまった
peg.romaji: Daiji na chiketto o, otoshite shimatta
peg.en: I went and dropped the important ticket.
peg.source: けいおん! (K-On!) — 平沢唯 (styled)

meaning: do in advance / leave as is
nuance: Do something ahead of time in preparation. Casual contraction: 〜とく.

examples.en: I'll book the hotel in advance before the trip.
Please leave the window open.
I looked up the company beforehand for the interview.

caution: Don't confuse it with 〜てある: ておく is doing something in advance on purpose, てある describes the resulting state that's been left in place.

peg.ja: 戦いの前に、体を休めておこう
peg.romaji: Tatakai no mae ni, karada o yasumete okō
peg.en: Before the fight, let's rest up in advance.
peg.source: ドラゴンボール (Dragon Ball) — 孫悟空 (styled)

nuance: Do something to see how it goes — a trial, not necessarily a struggle.

examples.en: I'll try eating this shop's ramen.
I tried calling the ward office.
I tried out the standing soba shop I'd been curious about.

caution: The みる here is auxiliary and written in kana (食べてみる), never in kanji (×食べて見る, which would mean "eat and look").

peg.ja: とりあえず、やってみよう！
peg.romaji: Toriaezu, yatte miyō!
peg.en: For now, let's just give it a try!
peg.source: 天元突破グレンラガン (Gurren Lagann) — シモン (styled)

nuance: You do something for someone else's benefit; can sound condescending, so use with care.

examples.en: I showed my friend the way.
I carried her luggage for her.
I showed a lost tourist which way to go.

caution: Never use てあげる about doing a favour for the listener (×荷物を持ってあげます to a boss) — it sounds patronising; use お〜しましょうか or humble 〜させていただく instead.

peg.ja: 困っているなら、手伝ってあげる
peg.romaji: Komatte iru nara, tetsudatte ageru
peg.en: If you're in trouble, I'll help you out.
peg.source: ドラえもん (Doraemon) — ドラえもん (styled)

nuance: Someone does something for me or my in-group; carries a sense of gratitude.

examples.en: The shop clerk showed me the way.
My friend came all the way to the airport for me.
My neighbor kindly taught me the trash-day rules.

caution: Direction is fixed: くれる is a favour coming TO you/your in-group, so the giver is the subject (友達が手伝ってくれた) — reversing it with あげる/もらう is the classic direction error.

peg.ja: 君が、僕を助けてくれた
peg.romaji: Kimi ga, boku o tasukete kureta
peg.en: You saved me.
peg.source: ソードアート・オンライン (SAO) — キリト (styled)

meaning: have someone do / receive the favor
nuance: I receive the favor of an action; the doer is marked with に.

examples.en: I had my friend help me move.
I had my teacher correct my essay.
I had the realtor find me a place with no guarantor needed.

caution: With てもらう the receiver is the subject and the doer takes に (友達に手伝ってもらった); confusing that に with the が of てくれる is the usual slip.

peg.ja: コーチに、フォームを直してもらった
peg.romaji: Kōchi ni, fōmu o naoshite moratta
peg.en: I had the coach fix my form.
peg.source: ハイキュー!! (Haikyu!!) — 日向翔陽 (styled)

meaning: go on doing / change away from now
nuance: A change moving away from the present into the future, or an action done then leaving.

examples.en: I'll keep studying Japanese from now on.
In the morning I'll buy tea at the konbini and head off.
Little by little, I'll grow used to life in Tokyo.

caution: For directional/aspectual meaning keep いく in kana (増えていく); pair it with 〜てくる by axis — くる = up to now, いく = from now on.

peg.ja: これからも、強くなっていく
peg.romaji: Kore kara mo, tsuyoku natte iku
peg.en: From here on too, I'll keep getting stronger.
peg.source: ONE PIECE — モンキー・D・ルフィ (styled)

meaning: come to / change up to now
nuance: A change that has developed up to the present, or going to do something and coming back.

examples.en: It has gradually gotten cold.
I'll go buy a bento at the konbini (and come back).
I've started to build confidence in Japanese conversation.

caution: Direction of change matters: 〜てくる develops up to now, 〜ていく from now on — 増えてきた (has increased) vs 増えていく (will keep increasing).

peg.ja: 修行のおかげで、力がついてきた
peg.romaji: Shugyō no okage de, chikara ga tsuite kita
peg.en: Thanks to the training, I've been gaining power.
peg.source: ドラゴンボール (Dragon Ball) — ベジータ (styled)

nuance: A transitive verb's result left in place on purpose; the object usually takes が.

examples.en: A map has been put up on the wall.
Beer has been chilled in the fridge.
My name is written on my things in the share-house fridge.

caution: てある uses a TRANSITIVE verb with が for a deliberately-left result (窓が開けてある); don't confuse it with ている on an intransitive verb (窓が開いている), which is just a natural state.

peg.ja: ドアに、貼り紙がしてある
peg.romaji: Doa ni, harigami ga shite aru
peg.en: There's a notice put up on the door.
peg.source: ドラえもん (Doraemon) — 野比のび太 (styled)

examples.en: I started learning Japanese last month.
The cherry blossoms have started to bloom.
From April, I started attending a Japanese language school.

nuance: Marks the onset of an action — you begin the verb itself. 始める is transitive/deliberate; the intransitive 始まる is for events that start on their own (映画が始まる).
caution: Attach to the ます-stem (降り始める, ×降る始める); use 〜始める (you start an action), not 始まる, which is for things that begin by themselves.

peg.ja: 強くなるため、今日から鍛え始める
peg.romaji: Tsuyoku naru tame, kyō kara kitae hajimeru
peg.en: To get stronger, I'll start training from today.
peg.source: ドラゴンボール (Dragon Ball) — 孫悟空 (styled)

examples.en: I finished reading the book.
Once I finish the homework, I'll sleep.
I finally finished unpacking my new room.

nuance: Marks finishing an action through to its end — "finish doing." Pairs naturally with 〜始める as the bookend of an activity.
caution: Attach to the ます-stem (食べ終わる, ×食べる終わる); it means you finish AN ACTION, so it doesn't fit sudden-end events the way 終わる-as-main-verb does.

peg.ja: やっと、宿題を書き終わった
peg.romaji: Yatto, shukudai o kaki owatta
peg.en: I finally finished writing my homework.
peg.source: ちびまる子ちゃん — さくらももこ (styled)

nuance: Conjugates like an i-adjective (〜やすかった). The full kanji 易い is rarely written.

examples.en: This pen is easy to write with.
Shinjuku station is easy to get lost in.
This station is easy to make transfers at.

caution: Attach to the ます-stem (使いやすい, ×使るやすい); it describes an inherent easiness, not "I am able to" — that's the potential form.

peg.ja: この技は、覚えやすいよ
peg.romaji: Kono waza wa, oboe yasui yo
peg.en: This move is easy to learn.
peg.source: ポケットモンスター (Pokémon) — サトシ (styled)

nuance: The opposite of 〜やすい; conjugates like an i-adjective.

examples.en: This kanji is hard to read.
The streets in front of the station are hard to figure out.
On a packed train it's hard to look at your phone.

caution: Attach to the ます-stem (読みにくい); for physical/inherent difficulty use にくい, but for difficulty from external circumstances Japanese often prefers 〜づらい or 〜がたい.

peg.ja: この道は、暗くて歩きにくい
peg.romaji: Kono michi wa, kurakute aruki nikui
peg.en: This road is dark and hard to walk on.
peg.source: となりのトトロ (My Neighbor Totoro) — 草壁サツキ (styled)

meaning: sometimes happens / there are times when
nuance: With a dictionary verb it means an occasional occurrence; do not confuse it with V-た + ことがある (past experience).

examples.en: There are times when I miss the last train.
Because of overtime, it sometimes gets late at night.
On weekends, there are times I drink until morning.

caution: Tense on the verb changes the meaning: dictionary form + ことがある = "sometimes happens," but past た + ことがある = "have done before (experience)" — don't mix them.

peg.ja: たまに、家が恋しくなることがある
peg.romaji: Tama ni, ie ga koishiku naru koto ga aru
peg.en: Sometimes I get homesick.
peg.source: ヴァイオレット・エヴァーガーデン (Violet Evergarden) — ヴァイオレット (styled)

connection: V-dict / V-ない + ことにする
nuance: The speaker's own decision. Contrast with ことになる, where the decision comes from outside.

examples.en: I've decided to study Japanese every morning.
I've decided not to eat out this month.
To save money, I've decided to cook for myself.

caution: ことにする is YOUR own decision; ことになる is a decision made by circumstances/others — swapping them reverses who chose.

peg.ja: 俺は、逃げないことにした
peg.romaji: Ore wa, nigenai koto ni shita
peg.en: I've decided not to run away.
peg.source: 進撃の巨人 (Attack on Titan) — エレン・イェーガー (styled)

nuance: The outcome is decided by circumstances or others, not the speaker's own choice.

examples.en: It's been decided that I'll work in Hokkaido from next month.
It's been decided that the meeting will be next week.
Starting next week, it's been arranged that I'll work at the Shinjuku store.

caution: ことになる signals the decision came from outside you, so don't use it for your own free choice (that's ことにする), even when you're the one affected.

peg.ja: 俺たち、一緒に旅をすることになった
peg.romaji: Oretachi, issho ni tabi o suru koto ni natta
peg.en: It's been decided that we'll travel together.
peg.source: 鋼の錬金術師 (Fullmetal Alchemist) — エドワード・エルリック (styled)

examples.en: I want to go to a ramen shop called "Ichiran."
I heard the word "share house" for the first time.
I ate a food called monja for the first time.

nuance: Introduces a name or label, quoting what something is called (田中という人 = a person called Tanaka). Also bundles a preceding clause into a noun-like idea.
caution: The naming という takes a plain quote before it (「みどり」という店); don't drop the と (×みどりいう) and don't confuse it with こと (the nominaliser).

peg.ja: エマという名前だ。よろしく
peg.romaji: Ema to iu namae da. Yoroshiku
peg.en: My name is Emma. Nice to meet you.
peg.source: 約束のネバーランド (The Promised Neverland) — エマ (styled)

nuance: Lists a couple of representative examples casually, implying there are others.

examples.en: At the convenience store I buy things like rice balls and tea.
On weekends I spend the time doing things like taking a walk.
On days off, I stroll around Ueno and Asakusa and places like that.

caution: とか is casual and implies an unfinished list ("things like…"), so don't use it in formal writing (use など) and don't treat it as an exact enumeration.

peg.ja: カラオケとか、行かない？
peg.romaji: Karaoke toka, ikanai?
peg.en: Wanna go do karaoke or something?
peg.source: らき☆すた (Lucky Star) — 泉こなた (styled)

examples.en: I showed them around Asakusa, Shibuya, and so on.
There are things like books on the desk.
At the 100-yen shop I bought plates, chopsticks, and so on.

nuance: Signals a non-exhaustive list — "…and the like." The more formal, written counterpart of casual とか.
caution: など follows the noun it lists (本や雑誌など), and reads なんか in casual speech — don't mistake it for the standalone 何 (なに).

peg.ja: 回復薬など、必要な物は揃えた
peg.romaji: Kaifukuyaku nado, hitsuyō na mono wa soroeta
peg.en: I've gathered what's needed, like healing potions and such.
peg.source: ソードアート・オンライン (SAO) — アスナ (styled)

nuance: Lists reasons or qualities, often implying "and on top of that."

examples.en: This room is cheap, and what's more it's close to the station.
It's raining today, so (and what's more) I'll stay home.
This room is close to the station, and the rent is cheap too.

caution: し attaches to the plain form (安いし、近いし) and implies "and, among other reasons," so listing just one し-clause still hints there are more.

peg.ja: 安いし、美味しいし、最高だ！
peg.romaji: Yasui shi, oishii shi, saikō da!
peg.en: It's cheap, and it's delicious — the best!
peg.source: 孤独のグルメ (Kodoku no Gurume) — 井之頭五郎 (styled)

nuance: Carries a sense of surprise, dissatisfaction, or unexpected contrast.

examples.en: Even though I made a reservation, the restaurant was closed.
Even though it's Sunday, the train is crowded.
Even though I'd made a reservation, the shop was closed.

caution: のに carries reproach or surprise, so don't use it for a neutral contrast (that's が/けど); after a noun/na-adjective it needs な (元気なのに, ×元気のに).

peg.ja: あんなに頑張ったのに、負けた
peg.romaji: Anna ni ganbatta noni, maketa
peg.en: Even though I tried so hard, I lost.
peg.source: ハイキュー!! (Haikyu!!) — 月島蛍 (styled)

nuance: Presents the reason as objective fact; softer and more polite than から.

examples.en: Because my head hurts, I'll go home early.
Because it's my first time, I don't know where the ward office is.
Since it's an unfamiliar area, I walked while checking a map.

caution: After a noun/na-adjective ので needs な (病気なので, ×病気だので); it presents the reason as objective fact, so it's odd before a direct command or invitation.

peg.ja: 体調が悪いので、早退させてください
peg.romaji: Taichō ga warui node, sōtai sasete kudasai
peg.en: Because I'm not feeling well, please let me leave early.
peg.source: 呪術廻戦 (Jujutsu Kaisen) — 虎杖悠仁 (styled)

nuance: A more formal, softer version of けど; connects two clauses with contrast.

examples.en: Japanese is difficult, however it's interesting.
I went to the izakaya in front of the station, but it was full.
I took the interview, but I still haven't heard back.

caution: けれども/けれど/けど form a politeness scale (formal→casual), so match the register — plain けど sounds too casual in a business email.

peg.ja: 努力はしましたけれども、及びませんでした
peg.romaji: Doryoku wa shimashita keredomo, oyobimasen deshita
peg.en: I did make the effort, but it wasn't enough.
peg.source: 進撃の巨人 (Attack on Titan) — エルヴィン・スミス (styled)

meaning: there is a (smell / sound / taste / feeling)
nuance: Used with perception nouns like におい, 音, 味, 気; describes something sensed, not deliberately done.

examples.en: There's a nice smell coming from the kitchen.
There's a strange sound coming from the room next door.
A great yakitori smell drifts out of this izakaya.

caution: It's built with が, not を (においがする, ×においをする), and only works with perception nouns (音・味・におい・気・感じ), not general nouns.

peg.ja: 何か、いい匂いがする
peg.romaji: Nanika, ii nioi ga suru
peg.en: Something smells good.
peg.source: 千と千尋の神隠し (Spirited Away) — 荻野千尋 (styled)

connection: V-ている / N-の + 間に
nuance: 間に marks a point within a span when something happens; 間 alone marks the whole duration.

examples.en: While I was sleeping, there was an earthquake.
During the summer break, I went to Hokkaido.
While I was out, a delivery had arrived.

caution: 間に marks a moment inside the span when a one-time event happens (留守の間に電話が来た); plain 間 marks an action lasting the WHOLE span — dropping the に changes the meaning.

peg.ja: 敵が寝ている間に、逃げよう
peg.romaji: Teki ga nete iru aida ni, nigeyō
peg.en: While the enemy is asleep, let's escape.
peg.source: ドラゴンボール (Dragon Ball) — クリリン (styled)

nuance: Marks a continuous action up to an end point. Contrast with までに (a deadline).

examples.en: I work my part-time job until ten at night.
I'll go to Shinjuku by train.
I stayed drinking at this place until the last train left.

caution: まで is continuous action up TO an endpoint (5時まで働く); it needs a durative verb, unlike までに, which pairs with a one-time completion.

peg.ja: 終わるまで、絶対に諦めない
peg.romaji: Owaru made, zettai ni akiramenai
peg.en: Until it's over, I'll never give up.
peg.source: ONE PIECE — ロロノア・ゾロ (styled)

nuance: A deadline by which an action must be completed. Contrast with まで (continuous up to a point).

examples.en: I'll submit the documents by Friday.
I want to get to the station by the last train.

ja.t: 払わなければならない

examples.en: I have to pay the rent by the end of this month.

caution: までに is a deadline for a one-time action (5時までに終わる), while まで is continuous action up to that time — using まで for a deadline (×5時まで終わる) is the classic error.

peg.ja: 夜明けまでに、片を付ける
peg.romaji: Yoake made ni, kata o tsukeru
peg.en: I'll settle this by dawn.
peg.source: 呪術廻戦 (Jujutsu Kaisen) — 五条悟 (styled)

connection: N + ばかり / V-て + ばかり
nuance: Emphasizes that it's always the same thing, often with a critical tone.

examples.en: Every day I eat nothing but ramen.
My little brother does nothing but play games.
Lately I've been eating nothing but konbini bento.

caution: Don't confuse noun+ばかり ("nothing but," often critical) with past た+ばかり ("just did") — same word, different structure and meaning.

peg.ja: こいつは、文句ばかり言う
peg.romaji: Koitsu wa, monku bakari iu
peg.en: This guy does nothing but complain.
peg.source: 銀魂 (Gintama) — 志村新八 (styled)

nuance: Only a short time has passed since the action, in the speaker's feeling. Contrast with たところ (objective timing).

examples.en: I just came to Japan, so I don't have any friends yet.
I just bought this smartphone.
I've only just arrived in Japan, so I don't know up from down.

caution: たばかり is subjective ("it feels like it just happened," even hours ago), whereas たところ is objective ("literally just now") — don't treat them as identical.

peg.ja: この街に、来たばかりなんだ
peg.romaji: Kono machi ni, kita bakari nanda
peg.en: I only just came to this town.
peg.source: 君の名は。(Your Name) — 宮水三葉 (styled)

meaning: about to / in the middle of / just did
connection: V-dict / V-ている / V-た + ところ
nuance: The verb form sets the timing: dict = about to, ている = in the middle of, た = just finished.

examples.en: I'm just about to leave the house now.
I've just finished eating lunch.
I'm right in the middle of writing my resume.

caution: The verb form sets the timing: 食べるところ (about to), 食べているところ (in the middle of), 食べたところ (just finished) — using the wrong tense shifts the whole meaning.

peg.ja: ちょうど今、出かけるところだ
peg.romaji: Chōdo ima, dekakeru tokoro da
peg.en: I'm just about to head out right now.
peg.source: 名探偵コナン (Detective Conan) — 服部平次 (styled)

nuance: A state remains unchanged while another action happens, often unintentionally.

examples.en: I fell asleep with the lights left on.
I went out leaving the window open.
I left the window open and went off to work.

caution: まま needs past た for a verb (つけたまま, ×つけるまま); it means a state was left unchanged, so it doesn't fit an ongoing action (that's ながら).

peg.ja: 電気を、つけたまま寝てしまった
peg.romaji: Denki o, tsuketa mama nete shimatta
peg.en: I fell asleep with the light still on.
peg.source: サザエさん — 磯野カツオ (styled)

nuance: The te-form of the negative; expresses a negative reason or cause for what follows.

examples.en: I couldn't read the kanji and so I was in trouble.
The train didn't come and so I was late.
I couldn't read the kanji, so I couldn't fill out the form.

caution: なくて links a negative cause/state (お金がなくて困った), but you can't make a negative REQUEST with it — "please don't…" needs 〜ないでください, not ×〜なくてください.

peg.ja: 君に会えなくて、寂しかった
peg.romaji: Kimi ni aenakute, samishikatta
peg.en: I couldn't meet you, and it was lonely.
peg.source: 君の名は。(Your Name) — 立花瀧 (styled)

examples.en: I don't know whether or not I passed the interview.
I'll check whether or not this shop is open.
I asked whether or not this shop takes cards.

nuance: Embeds a yes/no question ("whether or not…") inside a larger sentence. Use it when the buried question has no question word; use 疑問詞+か when it does.
caution: Use かどうか only for yes/no questions; when the embedded question already has a question word (どこ, 誰…), use just か, not ×どこかどうか.

peg.ja: 間に合うかどうか、賭けてみよう
peg.romaji: Maniau ka dō ka, kakete miyō
peg.en: Let's gamble on whether we'll make it in time.
peg.source: デスノート (Death Note) — 夜神月 (styled)

nuance: A question word plus か embeds a whole question inside a larger sentence.

examples.en: Please tell me where the station is.
I asked how much the rent is.
I asked the station attendant which train I should take.

caution: The embedded question keeps plain form and ends in か before the main verb (どこに行くか分からない); don't add です inside it (×どこに行きますか分からない).

peg.ja: 何が起きたのか、誰も知らない
peg.romaji: Nani ga okita no ka, dare mo shiranai
peg.en: No one knows what happened.
peg.source: 東京喰種 (Tokyo Ghoul) — 金木研 (styled)

connection: plain form + と言っていました
nuance: Reports what someone else said. と言っていました reports ongoing/general speech; と言いました a single utterance.

examples.en: The store manager said that tomorrow is a day off.
My friend said that Hokkaido is cold.
The landlord said they'll come for an inspection next week.

caution: と言っていました reports someone's ongoing/general remark; と言いました reports a single past utterance — and the quote before と stays in plain form.

peg.ja: 先生が、明日は休みだと言っていました
peg.romaji: Sensei ga, ashita wa yasumi da to itte imashita
peg.en: The teacher said that tomorrow is a day off.
peg.source: けいおん! (K-On!) — 秋山澪 (styled)

nuance: Expresses purpose with a volitional verb; with a noun it means "for the sake of."

examples.en: I save money for a trip.
In order to get a visa, I gather the documents.
I went to the immigration office in order to renew my visa.

caution: ために takes a VOLITIONAL, same-subject verb for purpose (合格するために勉強する); for a potential/negative or different-subject goal use ように instead.

peg.ja: 夢を叶えるために、上京してきた
peg.romaji: Yume o kanaeru tame ni, jōkyō shite kita
peg.en: I came to Tokyo in order to make my dream come true.
peg.source: NANA — 小松奈々 (styled)

nuance: Purpose with a non-volitional verb (potential or negative); contrast with ために, used with volitional verbs.

examples.en: I practice every day so that I can read kanji.
I leave early so that I don't miss the last train.
So I wouldn't miss the last train, I left the shop early.

caution: Purpose ように pairs with a non-volitional verb — potential or negative (見えるように, 忘れないように); swapping it with ために (volitional) is the classic ように-vs-ために mistake.

peg.ja: みんなが幸せになれるように、祈っている
peg.romaji: Minna ga shiawase ni nareru yō ni, inotte iru
peg.en: I'm praying so that everyone can be happy.
peg.source: 美少女戦士セーラームーン (Sailor Moon) — 月野うさぎ (styled)

meaning: I wish I had / should have
nuance: Expresses regret over something not done. Add なければ for regret over something that was done.

examples.en: I wish I had brought an umbrella.
I should have made a reservation sooner.
I should have brought an umbrella — it suddenly started raining.

caution: It expresses regret about the PAST, so keep よかった in the past tense (行けばよかった, ×行けばいい, which is present advice "you should go").

peg.ja: もっと早く、言えばよかった
peg.romaji: Motto hayaku, ieba yokatta
peg.en: I should have said it sooner.
peg.source: 四月は君の嘘 (Your Lie in April) — 有馬公生 (styled)

nuance: A soft suggestion or advice. Can sound pushy if overused with someone of higher status.

examples.en: If you're tired, how about resting a little?
How about trying to ask the station staff?
Why don't you ask the manager about things you don't understand?

caution: It's advice aimed at the listener, so it can sound bossy toward a superior; the たら here is fixed and can't be replaced by ば/と in this suggestion frame.

peg.ja: 少し、休んだらどう？
peg.romaji: Sukoshi, yasundara dō?
peg.en: How about resting a little?
peg.source: ドラえもん (Doraemon) — 源静香 (styled)

nuance: A non-contiguous pattern: そんなに sets up the degree, and the negative verb/adjective completes it.

examples.en: It's not that cold today.
This izakaya isn't that expensive.
My first paycheck wasn't all that much.

caution: そんなに must pair with a NEGATIVE (そんなに高くない = "not that expensive"); with an affirmative it flips to "that much / so very," a different meaning.

peg.ja: そんなに、怖くないよ
peg.romaji: Sonna ni, kowaku nai yo
peg.en: It's not that scary.
peg.source: となりのトトロ (My Neighbor Totoro) — 草壁メイ (styled)

nuance: A casual self-directed wondering, often spoken to oneself.

examples.en: I wonder if it'll be sunny tomorrow.
I wonder if this is the right train.
I wonder how you read this kanji.

caution: かな is casual self-talk, not a polite question to someone else; the male-leaning かなあ/かね differ in tone, and it takes plain form (行くかな, ×行きますかな).

peg.ja: 明日は、晴れるかな
peg.romaji: Ashita wa, hareru kana
peg.en: I wonder if it'll be sunny tomorrow.
peg.source: からかい上手の高木さん — 西片 (styled)

nuance: The plain-form conjecture; でしょう is its polite equivalent.

examples.en: It'll probably be crowded on the weekend.
He's probably already gone home.
At this hour, the shop is probably already crowded.

caution: Plain だろう is informal (でしょう is its polite form); a na-adjective/noun drops だ (静かだろう, 雨だろう — ×雨だだろう). Rising-intonation だろう？ seeks agreement.

peg.ja: この勝負、俺が勝つだろう
peg.romaji: Kono shōbu, ore ga katsu darō
peg.en: This match — I'll probably win it.
peg.source: 遊☆戯☆王 (Yu-Gi-Oh!) — 海馬瀬人 (styled)

nuance: Attaches to a quantity to mean "in equal portions" or "a certain amount at a time."

examples.en: I memorize a little kanji at a time every day.
Each person got two rice balls each.
Every day I memorize ten vocabulary words at a time.

caution: ずつ attaches to a quantity, not a bare noun (一つずつ, 少しずつ — ×りんごずつ), and means equal portions, so don't use it for a simple total.

peg.ja: 少しずつ、強くなればいい
peg.romaji: Sukoshi zutsu, tsuyoku nareba ii
peg.en: It's fine to get stronger little by little.
peg.source: 呪術廻戦 (Jujutsu Kaisen) — 伏黒恵 (styled)

nuance: Regular sonkeigo: raises the subject (a customer, a boss). The verb stem sits between お and になる — e.g. お読みになる (reads).

examples.en: The store manager has already gone home.
Will the president read this book?
What time will the store manager be back?

caution: お〜になる RAISES someone else's action (respect); never apply it to yourself — for your own action use the humble お〜する. Swapping the two is the classic keigo error.

peg.ja: 社長は、もうお帰りになりました
peg.romaji: Shachō wa, mō okaeri ni narimashita
peg.en: The president has already gone home.
peg.source: 黒執事 (Black Butler) — セバスチャン・ミカエリス (styled)

nuance: Regular kenjougo: lowers your own action toward someone you respect. お待ちする (I wait), お持ちする (I carry it for you).

examples.en: I'll carry your luggage (for you).
I'll wait for you in the lobby.
After the interview, we'll call you from our end.

caution: お〜する LOWERS your own action toward someone you respect; never use it for the other person's action (that's お〜になる) — reversing them is the classic keigo mistake.

peg.ja: お荷物、私がお持ちします
peg.romaji: Onimotsu, watashi ga omochi shimasu
peg.en: I'll carry your bags.
peg.source: サザエさん — 磯野フネ (styled)

reading: とくべつなそんけいご
meaning: special honorific verbs (いらっしゃる, 召し上がる…)
nuance: Irregular sonkeigo replaces whole verbs: いる/行く/来る→いらっしゃる, 食べる/飲む→召し上がる, 言う→おっしゃる, する→なさる, 見る→ご覧になる.

examples.en: What would you like to eat, sir/madam?
The teacher will come here tomorrow.
Two guests have arrived at the restaurant.

caution: These special honorifics are for OTHERS' actions only; pairing them with yourself, or double-marking (×お召し上がりになる done wrong), is the usual trap. Their humble counterparts (参る, いただく) are separate words.

peg.ja: 先生は、もういらっしゃいました
peg.romaji: Sensei wa, mō irasshaimashita
peg.en: The teacher has already arrived.
peg.source: 涼宮ハルヒの憂鬱 (Haruhi) — 朝比奈みくる (styled)

reading: とくべつなけんじょうご
meaning: special humble verbs (参る, いただく, 申す…)
nuance: Irregular kenjougo lowers your own action: 行く/来る→参る, 食べる/飲む/もらう→いただく, 言う→申す, する→いたす, 見る→拝見する.

examples.en: My name is Tanaka.
I will come to the office at ten o'clock.
I'll come to your company at nine o'clock tomorrow.

caution: These humble verbs lower YOUR OWN action, so never use them for the listener (×先生が申しました); use the honorific set (おっしゃる, いらっしゃる) for others.

peg.ja: 初めまして、田中と申します
peg.romaji: Hajimemashite, Tanaka to mōshimasu
peg.en: Nice to meet you; my name is Tanaka.
peg.source: スパイファミリー (Spy x Family) — ロイド・フォージャー (styled)

nuance: Polite request built on the sonkeigo frame — softer and more respectful than the plain 〜てください. お待ちください = please wait.

examples.en: Please wait a moment.
Please have a seat here.
Please write your name on this form.

caution: お〜ください uses the ます-stem (お待ちください, ×お待ってください) and is respectful; for irregular honorific verbs some use ご (ご覧ください), and する-verbs take ご (ご注意ください).

peg.ja: 少々、お待ちください
peg.romaji: Shōshō, omachi kudasai
peg.en: Please wait a moment.
peg.source: ご注文はうさぎですか? — 香風智乃 (styled)

nuance: The most polite copula — shop and hotel staff use it. でございます replaces です; ございます alone can replace あります.

examples.en: This is the new product.
Reception is on the second floor.
The reception desk is on the second floor.

caution: でございます is a polite copula ("is") replacing です; don't confuse it with plain ございます (replacing あります, "there is") — they're not interchangeable.

peg.ja: こちらが、当店の名物でございます
peg.romaji: Kochira ga, tōten no meibutsu de gozaimasu
peg.en: This is our shop's specialty.
peg.source: かぐや様は告らせたい (Kaguya-sama) — 早坂愛 (styled)

examples.en: This umbrella is the same as mine.
My room is the same size as his.
This set meal tastes the same as the shop back home.

nuance: States that two things are the same, marking the compared item with と (A と同じ). Follow it with a noun using 同じ〜 (同じ色) — no な.
caution: Compare with と, not に (私のと同じ, ×私のに同じ); 同じ modifies a noun directly with NO な (同じ本, ×同じな本).

peg.ja: 私も、あなたと同じ気持ちです
peg.romaji: Watashi mo, anata to onaji kimochi desu
peg.en: I feel the same as you.
peg.source: 四月は君の嘘 (Your Lie in April) — 宮園かをり (styled)

nuance: You can't state another person's inner feelings directly; がる reports the outward signs — 寒がる = looks/acts cold. Drops the final い/な of the adjective.

examples.en: My roommate always seems to feel cold.
The child is afraid of the dog.
My coworker is always missing the food from back home.

caution: Use がる for a THIRD person's outward feelings (弟は寒がる), not your own (say 寒い for yourself); it drops the final い/な (寂しい→寂しがる) and makes the feeling into a verb.

peg.ja: 妹が、新しい靴を欲しがっている
peg.romaji: Imōto ga, atarashii kutsu o hoshigatte iru
peg.en: My little sister wants new shoes.
peg.source: サザエさん — 磯野ワカメ (styled)

nuance: The がる of feelings applied to 〜たい: reports that a third person shows they want to do something. You use 〜たい for yourself, 〜たがる for others.

examples.en: My little brother wants to go to Akihabara.
My friend wanted to see the photos of the izakaya.
My friend wants to eat sushi at Tsukiji.

caution: たがる is for other people's desires (子供が行きたがる); use たい for yourself, and note the object often takes を where たい allows が (水を飲みたがる).

peg.ja: 子供が、外で遊びたがる
peg.romaji: Kodomo ga, soto de asobitagaru
peg.en: The kid wants to play outside.
peg.source: クレヨンしんちゃん (Crayon Shin-chan) — 野原しんのすけ (styled)

examples.en: Sorry for being late.
I'm sorry for calling so many times.
Sorry for asking so many times.

nuance: Apologises for the action just named — "sorry for (doing) X." The heavier 〜て申し訳ありません is its polite/formal upgrade.
caution: For apologising in advance about a request, use 〜てすみませんが or 恐れ入りますが; don't swap in 〜てありがとう, which thanks rather than apologises.

peg.ja: 遅れて、すみません
peg.romaji: Okurete, sumimasen
peg.en: Sorry for being late.
peg.source: ハイキュー!! (Haikyu!!) — 山口忠 (styled)

nuance: よかった is the past of いい — expresses relief/gladness that something happened. The negative 〜なくてよかった = glad I didn't.

examples.en: I'm glad I brought an umbrella.
I'm glad I moved into this share house.
I truly feel glad I came to Japan.

caution: It expresses relief about something that DID happen, so keep よかった past; the negative is 〜なくてよかった ("glad I didn't"), not ×〜ないでよかった as a rule.

peg.ja: 君に、会えてよかった
peg.romaji: Kimi ni, aete yokatta
peg.en: I'm glad I got to meet you.
peg.source: 君の名は。(Your Name) — 宮水三葉 (styled)

nuance: You want someone ELSE to do the action (marked with に). Contrast 〜たい (you want to do it yourself).

examples.en: I want the shop clerk to tell me the way.
I want the landlord to fix the air conditioner.
I want the landlord to fix the air conditioner.

caution: The person you want to act takes に (君に来てほしい); don't confuse it with 〜たい (you want to do it yourself) — subject and marking differ.

peg.ja: 俺のことを、忘れないでほしい
peg.romaji: Ore no koto o, wasurenaide hoshii
peg.en: I want you not to forget about me.
peg.source: 進撃の巨人 (Attack on Titan) — エレン・イェーガー (styled)

nuance: 必要 is a na-adjective/noun meaning "necessity". With a dictionary verb it states that doing X is required; the negative 必要はない = don't need to.

examples.en: You need to register your address at the ward office.
You need to write a résumé before the interview.
I need to open a bank account.

caution: Necessity 必要がある takes a dictionary verb (行く必要がある); the negative is 必要はない ("don't need to"), and 必要 is a noun, so a verb links directly, not with こと.

peg.ja: 真実を、知る必要がある
peg.romaji: Shinjitsu o, shiru hitsuyō ga aru
peg.en: There's a need to know the truth.
peg.source: PSYCHO-PASS サイコパス — 常守朱 (styled)

nuance: Different from 見られる/聞ける (ability): 見える/聞こえる mean something comes into view or reaches the ear on its own, without effort.

examples.en: You can see Tokyo Tower from the room's window.
I can hear music from the room next door.
From my room you can see the Tokyo Skytree.

caution: 見える/聞こえる = something naturally enters your sight/hearing; 見られる/聞ける = you have the ability/opportunity —富士山が見える (visible) vs 映画が見られる (able to watch) are not interchangeable.

peg.ja: 屋上から、富士山が見える
peg.romaji: Okujō kara, Fujisan ga mieru
peg.en: You can see Mt. Fuji from the rooftop.
peg.source: ゆるキャン△ (Laid-Back Camp) — 志摩リン (styled)

nuance: Sets up a request or problem gently, then trails off — the listener infers the ask. Common when approaching staff: "道に迷ったんですが…" (I'm lost, and so…).

examples.en: I've gotten lost — which way is the station?
I'd like to make a reservation — are you open tonight?
I'd like to open an account — what do I need?

caution: The trailing が softens and invites help; don't read it as the contrastive "but," and use のですが in polite/formal speech rather than the casual んだけど.

peg.ja: すみません、道に迷ったんですが…
peg.romaji: Sumimasen, michi ni mayotta ndesu ga…
peg.en: Excuse me, I've gotten lost, you see…
peg.source: 涼宮ハルヒの憂鬱 (Haruhi) — キョン (styled)

nuance: Placed after a number/amount, も stresses that it is surprisingly large. Contrast with しか…ない (only, surprisingly little).

examples.en: I went to as many as five interviews.
This coat cost a whole 30,000 yen.
After my first paycheck, I managed to save as much as 50,000 yen.

caution: Emphatic も follows a number to stress "as much as" (三時間も待った); its opposite is しか…ない ("only"), so don't mix the two when stressing amount.

peg.ja: あいつは、ケーキを五個も食べた
peg.romaji: Aitsu wa, kēki o goko mo tabeta
peg.en: That guy ate as many as five cakes.
peg.source: ドラえもん (Doraemon) — 剛田武 (styled)

connection: V-ない stem + なくてはいけません
nuance: Drop the ない, add なくてはいけません. Same "must" as なければならない; more conversational. The casual contraction is なくちゃ.

ja.t: 起きなくてはいけません
f: きなくてはいけません

examples.en: I have to get up early tomorrow.

ja.t: 分けなくてはいけません
f: けなくてはいけません

examples.en: You have to separate the trash.

ja.t: 出さなくてはいけません
f: さなくてはいけません

examples.en: You must put the trash out on the designated day.

caution: It's a double negative meaning "must," so don't read the ない as literal negation; keep the base verb's ない-stem (行かなくては…, ×行くなくては…).

peg.ja: 約束は、守らなくてはいけません
peg.romaji: Yakusoku wa, mamoranakute wa ikemasen
peg.en: You must keep your promises.
peg.source: ONE PIECE — ナミ (styled)

connection: casual contractions of なければ/なくては
nuance: なきゃ ← なければ, なくちゃ ← なくては. The trailing verb (いけない/ならない) is usually dropped: "もう行かなきゃ" = gotta go now.

examples.en: I gotta head home now.
I've gotta pay the rent.
It's the last train — I've gotta get home!

caution: These are spoken contractions of なければ/なくては with the いけない/ならない dropped — fine in casual talk, but write out the full form in formal or written Japanese.

peg.ja: 早く、宿題やらなくちゃ
peg.romaji: Hayaku, shukudai yaranakucha
peg.en: I gotta do my homework, quick.
peg.source: ちびまる子ちゃん — さくらももこ (styled)

nuance: 場合 = "situation/case"; presents a hypothetical or contingency, often on signs and instructions. 地震の場合は = in the event of an earthquake.

examples.en: In the case of an earthquake, please don't use the elevator.
If you're going to be late, please call the shop.
If the train is delayed, get a delay certificate.

caution: Nouns take の before 場合 (地震の場合), na-adjectives take な, and plain verbs attach directly (遅れる場合は); a bare だ (×地震だ場合) is wrong.

peg.ja: 地震の場合は、まず身を守れ
peg.romaji: Jishin no baai wa, mazu mi o mamore
peg.en: In the event of an earthquake, protect yourself first.
peg.source: PSYCHO-PASS サイコパス — 宜野座伸元 (styled)

nuance: Marks a degree or rough amount — "to the extent that" after a verb, or "about/approximately" after a quantity. Level is disputed between N4 and N3 lists, so filed at the lower boundary.

examples.en: It takes about ten minutes to the station.
My legs were so tired I could hardly stand.
This morning's train was so crowded I couldn't move.

caution: Don't confuse ほど's two jobs: after a quantity it's "about" (三日ほど), but in ば〜ほど or 〜ほど…ない it marks degree/comparison, not approximation.

peg.ja: 泣きたいほど、悔しい
peg.romaji: Nakitai hodo, kuyashii
peg.en: I'm frustrated to the point of wanting to cry.
peg.source: ハイキュー!! (Haikyu!!) — 影山飛雄 (styled)
